skill.name: clojure-write
skill.description: Guide Metabase Clojure and ClojureScript development with REPL-driven workflow, local linting, tests, formatting, and project conventions.
skill.summary: Guide Metabase Clojure changes with local REPL, lint, test, and style workflows.
skill.author: metabase

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many command-like patterns in SKILL.md. Review confirms these are mostly documentation examples for Metabase linting, testing, formatting, and REPL workflows, not hidden code execution or exfiltration. The skill is publishable with a warning because it guides agents to run local commands and evaluate Clojure code in a project REPL.

medium_findings.title: Local Development Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for external command guidance. The skill instructs agents to run Metabase lint, formatting, test, readability, and REPL commands. These commands are legitimate for a Clojure development skill, but they can execute project code and should run only in a trusted repository.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains explicit local command examples for linting, tests, formatting, and REPL evaluation. Context shows legitimate development use, with no evidence of malicious intent or network exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: REPL Evaluation Can Execute Project Code
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for risky capability in a legitimate context. The skill recommends evaluating Clojure expressions through the Metabase REPL. This supports development, but arbitrary REPL evaluation can trigger side effects if used on untrusted code or input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The REPL commands are directly documented and clearly intended for code evaluation. The confidence is high for capability risk, but lower than critical because the commands are local and development-focused.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The flagged lines contain descriptions, Clojure naming examples, Markdown conventions, TODO format guidance, or ordinary Clojure style prose. No weak cryptographic algorithm implementation or recommendation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line context does not contain cryptographic code, algorithm names, or security-sensitive hashing. The scanner appears to have matched short substrings inside normal documentation.

low_findings.title: Hard Link Static Finding Is A Shell Quoting False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The flagged filesystem line is a bash example that shows shell quoting inside a REPL command. It does not create a hard link or modify filesystem links.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside a fenced bash example and contains only a Metabase REPL command. No hard-link command or filesystem link operation is present.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The flagged lines discuss Clojure declarations, MBQL library use, and avoiding shell escaping issues. No system enumeration, host discovery, or environment probing was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context is normal project guidance. There is no command that gathers host, network, user, process, or environment information.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Local Command Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents local commands for Metabase linting, formatting, testing, readability checks, and REPL evaluation. This is expected for the skill, but users should review commands before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple explicit command examples are present. They are transparent and local, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Metabase Clojure changes require project-specific style, tests, and REPL workflow. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through those conventions.
actual_capabilities: Guides Metabase Clojure and ClojureScript naming, structure, and documentation conventions.
Recommends local lint, format, test, and readability commands used in Metabase development.
Explains when to prefer clojure-mcp tools over shell-based REPL commands.
Describes REST API, module, model, driver, and MBQL conventions for Metabase code.
Supports REPL-driven development with namespace loading and focused function evaluation.
limitations: It is tailored to the Metabase repository and may not fit other Clojure projects.
It does not provide executable helper scripts or automated refactoring tools.
It assumes local development commands are available in the target repository.
It cannot verify runtime behavior without access to the project environment.

use_cases.title: Implement Metabase Backend Changes
use_cases.description: Apply Metabase namespace, module, schema, model, and API conventions while adding Clojure backend features.

use_cases.title: Refactor Clojure Code Safely
use_cases.description: Use REPL checks, readability checks, and targeted tests while restructuring Clojure functions or namespaces.

use_cases.title: Prepare Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Run focused linting, formatting, and tests before submitting Metabase Clojure or ClojureScript changes.

prompt_templates.title: Follow Project Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to review this Metabase Clojure file for naming, docstrings, visibility, and formatting conventions.

prompt_templates.title: Add A Tested Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to add a small Clojure function, write a focused test, and identify the targeted lint and test commands.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor With REPL Checks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to refactor this namespace in small steps, checking readability and REPL behavior after each change.

prompt_templates.title: Design A Metabase Module Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the clojure-write skill to plan a Metabase module change with namespace placement, schemas, REST API tests, and validation commands.

output_examples.input: Review a new Metabase REST API endpoint.
output_examples.output: A checklist covering response schemas, Malli parameters, route naming, side effects, namespace placement, and required endpoint tests.
A step-by-step refactor plan with smaller pure functions, REPL validation points, readability checks, and targeted test suggestions.
A concise validation sequence covering updated lint checks, file formatting, targeted tests, and docstring review.

best_practices: Use clojure-mcp tools before shell REPL commands when they are available.
Run focused lint, formatting, readability, and test checks after changing Clojure code.
Keep functions small, documented when public, and aligned with Metabase namespace conventions.
anti_patterns: Do not run REPL commands against untrusted repositories or unreviewed input.
Do not bypass Metabase module linters with broad clj-kondo ignore comments.
Do not add REST API endpoints without schemas, parameter validation, and tests.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents write, refactor, lint, format, test, and evaluate Metabase Clojure and ClojureScript code.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: The skill documents local commands. The agent or user still decides when commands are run.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill recommends local shell and REPL commands. These are useful, but they can execute project code.

faq.question: Is there evidence of malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The file contains transparent development guidance and no network exfiltration patterns.

faq.question: Can this be used outside Metabase?
faq.answer: Some Clojure advice is general, but many commands and conventions are specific to Metabase.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
